question:
Let $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$  and  $b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n$
be nonnegative real numbers.
Show that
\begin{align*}
& (a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n)^{1/n} + (b_1 b_2 \cdots b_n)^{1/n} \\
&\leq [(a_1+b_1) (a_2+b_2) \cdots (a_n + b_n) ]^{1/n}.
\end{align*}
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Assume without loss of generality that $a_i + b_i > 0$
for each $i$ (otherwise both sides of the desired inequality are zero).
Then the AM-GM inequality gives
\begin{multline*}
\left( \frac{a_1\cdots a_n}{(a_1+b_1)\cdots(a_n+b_n)} \right)^{1/n} \\
\leq \frac{1}{n} \left( \frac{a_1}{a_1 + b_1} + \cdots + \frac{a_n}{a_n+b_n}
\right),
\end{multline*}
and likewise with the roles of $a$ and $b$ reversed. Adding these two
inequalities and clearing denominators yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write the desired inequality in the form
\[
(a_1 + b_1)\cdots(a_n+b_n) \geq
[(a_1\cdots a_n)^{1/n} + (b_1\cdots b_n)^{1/n}]^n,
\]
expand both sides, and compare the terms on both sides
in which $k$ of the terms are among the $a_i$. On the left,
one has the product of each $k$-element subset of $\{1, \dots, n\}$;
on the right, one has
\[
\binom{n}{k} (a_1\cdots a_n)^{k/n} \cdots (b_1 \dots b_n)^{(n-k)/n},
\]
which is precisely $\binom{n}{k}$ times the geometric mean of the terms
on the left.  Thus AM-GM shows that the terms under consideration on the
left exceed those on the right; adding these inequalities over all $k$
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Since both sides are continuous in each $a_i$, it is sufficient to
prove the claim with $a_1, \dots, a_n$ all positive (the general case
follows by taking limits as some of the $a_i$ tend to zero).
Put $r_i = b_i/a_i$; then the given inequality is equivalent to
\[
(1 + r_1)^{1/n} \cdots (1+r_n)^{1/n} \geq 1 + (r_1\cdots r_n)^{1/n}.
\]
In terms of the function
\[
f(x) = \log(1 + e^x)
\]
and the quantities $s_i = \log r_i$,
we can rewrite the desired inequality as
\[
\frac{1}{n}(f(s_1) + \cdots + f(s_n)) \geq f\left( \frac{s_1 + \cdots +
s_n}{n} \right).
\]
This will follow from Jensen's inequality if we can verify that $f$
is a convex function; it is enough to check that $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x$.
In fact,
\[
f'(x) = \frac{e^x}{1+e^x} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^x}
\]
is an increasing function of $x$, so $f''(x) > 0$ and Jensen's inequality
thus yields the desired result. (As long as the $a_i$ are all positive,
equality holds when $s_1 = \cdots = s_n$, i.e., when the vectors
$(a_1, \dots, a_n)$ and $(b_1, \dots, b_n)$. Of course other equality
cases crop up if some of the $a_i$ vanish, i.e., if $a_1=b_1=0$.)

\textbf{Fourth solution:}
We apply induction on $n$, the case $n=1$ being evident.
First we verify the auxiliary inequality
\[
(a^n + b^n)(c^n + d^n)^{n-1} \geq (ac^{n-1} + b d^{n-1})^n
\]
for $a,b,c,d \geq 0$.
The left side can be written as
\begin{align*}
a^n c^{n(n-1)} &+ b^n d^{n(n-1)} \\
&+ \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{i} b^n c^{ni} d^{n(n-1-i)} \\
&+ \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{i-1} a^n c^{n(i-1)} d^{n(n-i)}.
\end{align*}
Applying the weighted AM-GM inequality between matching terms in the two
sums yields
\begin{multline*}
(a^n + b^n)(c^n + d^n)^{n-1} \geq a^n c^{n(n-1)} + b^n d^{n(n-1)} \\
+ \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \binom{n}{i} a^i b^{n-i} c^{(n-1)i} d^{(n-1)(n-i)},
\end{multline*}
proving the auxiliary inequality.

Now given the auxiliary inequality and the $n-1$ case of the desired
inequality, we apply the auxiliary inequality with $a = a_1^{1/n}$,
$b = b_1^{1/n}$, $c = (a_2 \cdots a_n)^{1/n(n-1)}$,
$d = (b_2 \dots b_n)^{1/n(n-1)}$. The right side will be the $n$-th
power of the desired inequality. The left side comes out to
\[
(a_1 + b_1)((a_2 \cdots a_n)^{1/(n-1)} + (b_2 \cdots b_n)^{1/(n-1)})^{n-1},
\]
and by the induction hypothesis, the second factor is less than
$(a_2 + b_2)\cdots(a_n+b_n)$. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Note:}
Equality holds if and only if $a_i=b_i=0$ for some $i$ or if the vectors
$(a_1, \dots, a_n)$ and $(b_1, \dots, b_n)$ are proportional.
As pointed out by Naoki Sato, the problem also appeared on the 1992
Irish Mathematical Olympiad.
It is also a special case of a classical inequality,
known as H\"older's inequality, which generalizes the
Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (this is visible from the $n=2$ case); the
first solution above is adapted from the standard proof of H\"older's
inequality.
We don't know whether the declaration
``Apply H\"older's inequality'' by itself is considered
an acceptable solution to this problem.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $firsta, seconda, \dots, finala$  and  $firstb, secondb, \dots, finalb$
be nonnegative real numbers.
Show that
\begin{align*}
& (firsta seconda \cdots finala)^{1/countn} + (firstb secondb \cdots finalb)^{1/countn} \\
&\leq [(firsta+firstb) (seconda+secondb) \cdots (finala + finalb) ]^{1/countn}.
\end{align*}
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Assume without loss of generality that $indexa + indexb > 0$
for each $indice$ (otherwise both sides of the desired inequality are zero).
Then the AM-GM inequality gives
\begin{multline*}
\left( \frac{firsta\cdots finala}{(firsta+firstb)\cdots(finala+finalb)} \right)^{1/countn} \\
\leq \frac{1}{countn} \left( \frac{firsta}{firsta + firstb} + \cdots + \frac{finala}{finala+finalb}
\right),
\end{multline*}
and likewise with the roles of $indexa$ and $indexb$ reversed. Adding these two
inequalities and clearing denominators yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write the desired inequality in the form
\[
(firsta + firstb)\cdots(finala+finalb) \geq
[(firsta\cdots finala)^{1/countn} + (firstb\cdots finalb)^{1/countn}]^{countn},
\]
expand both sides, and compare the terms on both sides
in which $countk$ of the terms are among the $indexa$. On the left,
one has the product of each $countk$-element subset of $\{1, \dots, countn\}$;
on the right, one has
\[
\binom{countn}{countk} (firsta\cdots finala)^{countk/countn} \cdots (firstb \dots finalb)^{(countn-countk)/countn},
\]
which is precisely $\binom{countn}{countk}$ times the geometric mean of the terms
on the left.  Thus AM-GM shows that the terms under consideration on the
left exceed those on the right; adding these inequalities over all $countk$
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Since both sides are continuous in each $indexa$, it is sufficient to
prove the claim with $firsta, \dots, finala$ all positive (the general case
follows by taking limits as some of the $indexa$ tend to zero).
Put $ratioi = indexb/indexa$; then the given inequality is equivalent to
\[
(1 + r_1)^{1/countn} \cdots (1+r_{countn})^{1/countn} \geq 1 + (r_1\cdots r_{countn})^{1/countn}.
\]
In terms of the function
\[
logfun(realvar) = \log(1 + e^{realvar})
\]
and the quantities $logval = \log ratioi$,
we can rewrite the desired inequality as
\[
\frac{1}{countn}(logfun(s_1) + \cdots + logfun(s_{countn})) \geq logfun\left( \frac{s_1 + \cdots +
s_{countn}}{countn} \right).
\]
This will follow from Jensen's inequality if we can verify that $logfun$
is a convex function; it is enough to check that $logfun''(realvar) > 0$ for all $realvar$.
In fact,
\[
logfun'(realvar) = \frac{e^{realvar}}{1+e^{realvar}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{realvar}}
\]
is an increasing function of $realvar$, so $logfun''(realvar) > 0$ and Jensen's inequality
thus yields the desired result. (As long as the $indexa$ are all positive,
equality holds when $s_1 = \cdots = s_{countn}$, i.e., when the vectors
$(firsta, \dots, finala)$ and $(firstb, \dots, finalb)$. Of course other equality
cases crop up if some of the $indexa$ vanish, i.e., if $firsta=firstb=0$.)

\textbf{Fourth solution:}
We apply induction on $countn$, the case $countn=1$ being evident.
First we verify the auxiliary inequality
\[
(varalph^{countn} + varbeta^{countn})(vargamm^{countn} + vardelt^{countn})^{countn-1} \geq (varalph vargamm^{countn-1} + varbeta vardelt^{countn-1})^{countn}
\]
for $varalph,varbeta,vargamm,vardelt \geq 0$.
The left side can be written as
\begin{align*}
varalph^{countn} vargamm^{countn(countn-1)} &+ varbeta^{countn} vardelt^{countn(countn-1)} \\
&+ \sum_{\indice=1}^{countn-1} \binom{countn-1}{\indice} varbeta^{countn} vargamm^{countn\indice} vardelt^{countn(countn-1-\indice)} \\
&+ \sum_{\indice=1}^{countn-1} \binom{countn-1}{\indice-1} varalph^{countn} vargamm^{countn(\indice-1)} vardelt^{countn(countn-\indice)}.
\end{align*}
Applying the weighted AM-GM inequality between matching terms in the two
sums yields
\begin{multline*}
(varalph^{countn} + varbeta^{countn})(vargamm^{countn} + vardelt^{countn})^{countn-1} \geq varalph^{countn} vargamm^{countn(countn-1)} + varbeta^{countn} vardelt^{countn(countn-1)} \\
+ \sum_{\indice=1}^{countn-1} \binom{countn}{\indice} varalph^{\indice} varbeta^{countn-\indice} vargamm^{(countn-1)\indice} vardelt^{(countn-1)(countn-\indice)},
\end{multline*}
proving the auxiliary inequality.

Now given the auxiliary inequality and the $countn-1$ case of the desired
inequality, we apply the auxiliary inequality with $varalph = firsta^{1/countn}$,
$varbeta = firstb^{1/countn}$, $vargamm = (seconda \cdots finala)^{1/countn(countn-1)}$,
$vardelt = (secondb \dots finalb)^{1/countn(countn-1)}$. The right side will be the $countn$-th
power of the desired inequality. The left side comes out to
\[
(firsta + firstb)((seconda \cdots finala)^{1/(countn-1)} + (secondb \cdots finalb)^{1/(countn-1)})^{countn-1},
\]
and by the induction hypothesis, the second factor is less than
$(seconda + secondb)\cdots(finala+finalb)$. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Note:}
Equality holds if and only if $indexa=indexb=0$ for some $indice$ or if the vectors
$(firsta, \dots, finala)$ and $(firstb, \dots, finalb)$ are proportional.
As pointed out by Naoki Sato, the problem also appeared on the 1992
Irish Mathematical Olympiad.
It is also a special case of a classical inequality,
known as H\"older's inequality, which generalizes the
Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (this is visible from the $countn=2$ case); the
first solution above is adapted from the standard proof of H\"older's
inequality.
We don't know whether the declaration
``Apply H\"older's inequality'' by itself is considered
an acceptable solution to this problem.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Assume without loss of generality that $turnpike + riverbank > 0$ for each $rainstorm$ (otherwise both sides of the desired inequality are zero).
Then the AM-GM inequality gives
\begin{multline*}
\left( \frac{oceanview\cdots marshland}{(oceanview+driftwood)\cdots(marshland+starlight)} \right)^{1/willowtree} \\
\leq \frac{1}{willowtree} \left( \frac{oceanview}{oceanview + driftwood} + \cdots + \frac{marshland}{marshland+starlight}
\right),
\end{multline*}
and likewise with the roles of $sunflower$ and $thunderbolt$ reversed. Adding these two inequalities and clearing denominators yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write the desired inequality in the form
\[
(oceanview + driftwood)\cdots(marshland+starlight) \geq
[(oceanview\cdots marshland)^{1/willowtree} + (driftwood\cdots starlight)^{1/willowtree}]^{willowtree},
\]
expand both sides, and compare the terms on both sides in which $snowflake$ of the terms are among the $turnpike$. On the left, one has the product of each $snowflake$-element subset of $\{1, \dots, willowtree\}$; on the right, one has
\[
\binom{willowtree}{snowflake} (oceanview\cdots marshland)^{snowflake/willowtree} \cdots (driftwood \dots starlight)^{(willowtree-snowflake)/willowtree},
\]
which is precisely $\binom{willowtree}{snowflake}$ times the geometric mean of the terms on the left.  Thus AM-GM shows that the terms under consideration on the left exceed those on the right; adding these inequalities over all $snowflake$ yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Since both sides are continuous in each $turnpike$, it is sufficient to prove the claim with $oceanview, \dots, marshland$ all positive (the general case follows by taking limits as some of the $turnpike$ tend to zero).
Put $goldfinch = riverbank/turnpike$; then the given inequality is equivalent to
\[
(1 + r_1)^{1/willowtree} \cdots (1+r_n)^{1/willowtree} \geq 1 + (r_1\cdots r_n)^{1/willowtree}.
\]
In terms of the function
\[
meadowlark(cinnamon) = \log(1 + e^{cinnamon})
\]
and the quantities $trailhead = \log goldfinch$, we can rewrite the desired inequality as
\[
\frac{1}{willowtree}(meadowlark(s_1) + \cdots + meadowlark(s_n)) \geq meadowlark\left( \frac{s_1 + \cdots + s_n}{willowtree} \right).
\]
This will follow from Jensen's inequality if we can verify that $meadowlark$ is a convex function; it is enough to check that $meadowlark''(cinnamon) > 0$ for all $cinnamon$. In fact,
\[
meadowlark'(cinnamon) = \frac{e^{cinnamon}}{1+e^{cinnamon}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{cinnamon}}
\]
is an increasing function of $cinnamon$, so $meadowlark''(cinnamon) > 0$ and Jensen's inequality thus yields the desired result. (As long as the $turnpike$ are all positive, equality holds when $s_1 = \cdots = s_n$, i.e., when the vectors $(oceanview, \dots, marshland)$ and $(driftwood, \dots, starlight)$. Of course other equality cases crop up if some of the $turnpike$ vanish, i.e., if $oceanview=driftwood=0$.)

\textbf{Fourth solution:}
We apply induction on $willowtree$, the case $willowtree=1$ being evident.
First we verify the auxiliary inequality
\[
(sunflower^{willowtree} + thunderbolt^{willowtree})(moonstone^{willowtree} + pebblestone^{willowtree})^{willowtree-1} \geq (sunflower moonstone^{willowtree-1} + thunderbolt pebblestone^{willowtree-1})^{willowtree}
\]
for $sunflower, thunderbolt, moonstone, pebblestone \geq 0$.
The left side can be written as
\begin{align*}
sunflower^{willowtree} moonstone^{willowtree(willowtree-1)} &+ thunderbolt^{willowtree} pebblestone^{willowtree(willowtree-1)} \\
&+ \sum_{rainstorm=1}^{willowtree-1} \binom{willowtree-1}{rainstorm} thunderbolt^{willowtree} moonstone^{willowtree rainstorm} pebblestone^{willowtree(willowtree-1-rainstorm)} \\
&+ \sum_{rainstorm=1}^{willowtree-1} \binom{willowtree-1}{rainstorm-1} sunflower^{willowtree} moonstone^{willowtree(rainstorm-1)} pebblestone^{willowtree(willowtree-rainstorm)}.
\end{align*}
Applying the weighted AM-GM inequality between matching terms in the two sums yields
\begin{multline*}
(sunflower^{willowtree} + thunderbolt^{willowtree})(moonstone^{willowtree} + pebblestone^{willowtree})^{willowtree-1} \geq sunflower^{willowtree} moonstone^{willowtree(willowtree-1)} + thunderbolt^{willowtree} pebblestone^{willowtree(willowtree-1)} \\
+ \sum_{rainstorm=1}^{willowtree-1} \binom{willowtree}{rainstorm} sunflower^{rainstorm} thunderbolt^{willowtree-rainstorm} moonstone^{(willowtree-1)rainstorm} pebblestone^{(willowtree-1)(willowtree-rainstorm)},
\end{multline*}
proving the auxiliary inequality.

Now given the auxiliary inequality and the $willowtree-1$ case of the desired inequality, we apply the auxiliary inequality with $sunflower = oceanview^{1/willowtree}$,
$thunderbolt = driftwood^{1/willowtree}$, $moonstone = (lighthouse \cdots marshland)^{1/willowtree(willowtree-1)}$, $pebblestone = (hummingbird \dots starlight)^{1/willowtree(willowtree-1)}$. The right side will be the $willowtree$-th power of the desired inequality. The left side comes out to
\[
(oceanview + driftwood)((lighthouse \cdots marshland)^{1/(willowtree-1)} + (hummingbird \cdots starlight)^{1/(willowtree-1)})^{\!\, willowtree-1},
\]
and by the induction hypothesis, the second factor is less than $(lighthouse + hummingbird)\cdots(marshland+starlight)$. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Note:}
Equality holds if and only if $turnpike=riverbank=0$ for some $rainstorm$ or if the vectors $(oceanview, \dots, marshland)$ and $(driftwood, \dots, starlight)$ are proportional. As pointed out by Naoki Sato, the problem also appeared on the 1992 Irish Mathematical Olympiad. It is also a special case of a classical inequality, known as H"older's inequality, which generalizes the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (this is visible from the $willowtree=2$ case); the first solution above is adapted from the standard proof of H"older's inequality.
We don't know whether the declaration ``Apply H"older's inequality'' by itself is considered an acceptable solution to this problem.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $negativerealone, negativerealtwo, \dots, negativerealn$  and  $imaginaryone, imaginarytwo, \dots, imaginaryn$
be nonnegative real numbers.
Show that
\begin{align*}
& (negativerealone negativerealtwo \cdots negativerealn)^{1/\infinitesize} + (imaginaryone imaginarytwo \cdots imaginaryn)^{1/\infinitesize} \\
&\leq [(negativerealone+imaginaryone) (negativerealtwo+imaginarytwo) \cdots (negativerealn + imaginaryn) ]^{1/\infinitesize}.
\end{align*}
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Assume without loss of generality that $negativerealvar + imaginaryvar > 0$
for each $wholeindex$ (otherwise both sides of the desired inequality are zero).
Then the AM-GM inequality gives
\begin{multline*}
\left( \frac{negativerealone\cdots negativerealn}{(negativerealone+imaginaryone)\cdots(negativerealn+imaginaryn)} \right)^{1/\infinitesize} \\
\leq \frac{1}{\infinitesize} \left( \frac{negativerealone}{negativerealone + imaginaryone} + \cdots + \frac{negativerealn}{negativerealn+imaginaryn}
\right),
\end{multline*}
and likewise with the roles of $endvalue$ and $startvalue$ reversed. Adding these two
inequalities and clearing denominators yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write the desired inequality in the form
\[
(negativerealone + imaginaryone)\cdots(negativerealn+imaginaryn) \geq
[(negativerealone\cdots negativerealn)^{1/\infinitesize} + (imaginaryone\cdots imaginaryn)^{1/\infinitesize}]^{\infinitesize},
\]
expand both sides, and compare the terms on both sides
in which $\totalcount$ of the terms are among the $negativerealvar$. On the left,
one has the product of each $\totalcount$-element subset of $\{1, \dots, \infinitesize\}$;
on the right, one has
\[
\binom{\infinitesize}{\totalcount} (negativerealone\cdots negativerealn)^{\totalcount/\infinitesize} \cdots (imaginaryone \dots imaginaryn)^{(\infinitesize-\totalcount)/\infinitesize},
\]
which is precisely $\binom{\infinitesize}{\totalcount}$ times the geometric mean of the terms
on the left.  Thus AM-GM shows that the terms under consideration on the
left exceed those on the right; adding these inequalities over all $\totalcount$
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Since both sides are continuous in each $negativerealvar$, it is sufficient to
prove the claim with $negativerealone, \dots, negativerealn$ all positive (the general case
follows by taking limits as some of the $negativerealvar$ tend to zero).
Put $productindex = imaginaryvar/negativerealvar$; then the given inequality is equivalent to
\[
(1 + r_1)^{1/\infinitesize} \cdots (1+r_n)^{1/\infinitesize} \geq 1 + (r_1\cdots r_n)^{1/\infinitesize}.
\]
In terms of the function
\[
constantvalue(fixedconstant) = \log(1 + e^{fixedconstant})
\]
and the quantities $exponentindex = \log r_i$,
we can rewrite the desired inequality as
\[
\frac{1}{\infinitesize}(constantvalue(s_1) + \cdots + constantvalue(s_n)) \geq constantvalue\left( \frac{s_1 + \cdots +
s_n}{\infinitesize} \right).
\]
This will follow from Jensen's inequality if we can verify that $constantvalue$
is a convex function; it is enough to check that $constantvalue''(fixedconstant) > 0$ for all $fixedconstant$.
In fact,
\[
constantvalue'(fixedconstant) = \frac{e^{fixedconstant}}{1+e^{fixedconstant}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{fixedconstant}}
\]
is an increasing function of $fixedconstant$, so $constantvalue''(fixedconstant) > 0$ and Jensen's inequality
thus yields the desired result. (As long as the $negativerealvar$ are all positive,
equality holds when $s_1 = \cdots = s_n$, i.e., when the vectors
$(negativerealone, \dots, negativerealn)$ and $(imaginaryone, \dots, imaginaryn)$. Of course other equality
cases crop up if some of the $negativerealvar$ vanish, i.e., if $negativerealone=imaginaryone=0$.)

\textbf{Fourth solution:}
We apply induction on $\infinitesize$, the case $\infinitesize=1$ being evident.
First we verify the auxiliary inequality
\[
(endvalue^{\infinitesize} + startvalue^{\infinitesize})(inputvalue^{\infinitesize} + outputvalue^{\infinitesize})^{\infinitesize-1} \geq (endvalue inputvalue^{\infinitesize-1} + startvalue outputvalue^{\infinitesize-1})^{\infinitesize}
\]
for $endvalue,startvalue,inputvalue,outputvalue \geq 0$.
The left side can be written as
\begin{align*}
endvalue^{\infinitesize} inputvalue^{\infinitesize(\infinitesize-1)} &+ startvalue^{\infinitesize} outputvalue^{\infinitesize(\infinitesize-1)} \\
&+ \sum_{wholeindex=1}^{\infinitesize-1} \binom{\infinitesize-1}{wholeindex} startvalue^{\infinitesize} inputvalue^{\infinitesize wholeindex} outputvalue^{\infinitesize(\infinitesize-1-wholeindex)} \\
&+ \sum_{wholeindex=1}^{\infinitesize-1} \binom{\infinitesize-1}{wholeindex-1} endvalue^{\infinitesize} inputvalue^{\infinitesize(wholeindex-1)} outputvalue^{\infinitesize(\infinitesize-wholeindex)}.
\end{align*}
Applying the weighted AM-GM inequality between matching terms in the two
sums yields
\begin{multline*}
(endvalue^{\infinitesize} + startvalue^{\infinitesize})(inputvalue^{\infinitesize} + outputvalue^{\infinitesize})^{\infinitesize-1} \geq endvalue^{\infinitesize} inputvalue^{\infinitesize(\infinitesize-1)} + startvalue^{\infinitesize} outputvalue^{\infinitesize(\infinitesize-1)} \\
+ \sum_{wholeindex=1}^{\infinitesize-1} \binom{\infinitesize}{wholeindex} endvalue^{wholeindex} startvalue^{\infinitesize-wholeindex} inputvalue^{(\infinitesize-1)wholeindex} outputvalue^{(\infinitesize-1)(\infinitesize-wholeindex)},
\end{multline*}
proving the auxiliary inequality.

Now given the auxiliary inequality and the $\infinitesize-1$ case of the desired
inequality, we apply the auxiliary inequality with $endvalue = negativerealone^{1/\infinitesize}$,
$startvalue = imaginaryone^{1/\infinitesize}$, $inputvalue = (negativerealtwo \cdots negativerealn)^{1/\infinitesize(\infinitesize-1)}$,
$outputvalue = (imaginarytwo \dots imaginaryn)^{1/\infinitesize(\infinitesize-1)}$. The right side will be the $\infinitesize$-th
power of the desired inequality. The left side comes out to
\[
(negativerealone + imaginaryone)((negativerealtwo \cdots negativerealn)^{1/(\infinitesize-1)} + (imaginarytwo \cdots imaginaryn)^{1/(\infinitesize-1)})^{\infinitesize-1},
\]
and by the induction hypothesis, the second factor is less than
$(negativerealtwo + imaginarytwo)\cdots(negativerealn+imaginaryn)$. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Note:}
Equality holds if and only if $negativerealvar=imaginaryvar=0$ for some $wholeindex$ or if the vectors
$(negativerealone, \dots, negativerealn)$ and $(imaginaryone, \dots, imaginaryn)$ are proportional.
As pointed out by Naoki Sato, the problem also appeared on the 1992
Irish Mathematical Olympiad.
It is also a special case of a classical inequality,
known as H"older's inequality, which generalizes the
Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (this is visible from the $\infinitesize=2$ case); the
first solution above is adapted from the standard proof of H"older's
inequality.
We don't know whether the declaration
``Apply H"older's inequality'' by itself is considered
an acceptable solution to this problem.

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, \dots, mldpqrvo$  and  $rtyusfgh, eopncvxz, \dots, iyklmwer$
be nonnegative real numbers.
Show that
\begin{align*}
& (qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \cdots mldpqrvo)^{1/tbruqkse} + (rtyusfgh eopncvxz \cdots iyklmwer)^{1/tbruqkse} \\
&\leq [(qzxwvtnp+rtyusfgh) (hjgrksla+eopncvxz) \cdots (mldpqrvo + iyklmwer) ]^{1/tbruqkse}.
\end{align*}
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Assume without loss of generality that $xkceqbzn + szadqplm > 0$
for each $ucgmzrlo$ (otherwise both sides of the desired inequality are zero).
Then the AM-GM inequality gives
\begin{multline*}
\left( \frac{qzxwvtnp\cdots mldpqrvo}{(qzxwvtnp+rtyusfgh)\cdots(mldpqrvo+iyklmwer)} \right)^{1/tbruqkse} \\
\leq \frac{1}{tbruqkse} \left( \frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp + rtyusfgh} + \cdots + \frac{mldpqrvo}{mldpqrvo+iyklmwer}
\right),
\end{multline*}
and likewise with the roles of $nfqsyhjt$ and $pxladrwo$ reversed. Adding these two
inequalities and clearing denominators yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write the desired inequality in the form
\[
(qzxwvtnp + rtyusfgh)\cdots(mldpqrvo+iyklmwer) \geq
[(qzxwvtnp\cdots mldpqrvo)^{1/tbruqkse} + (rtyusfgh\cdots iyklmwer)^{1/tbruqkse}]^{tbruqkse},
\]
expand both sides, and compare the terms on both sides
in which $wirbpxsa$ of the terms are among the $xkceqbzn$. On the left,
one has the product of each $wirbpxsa$-element subset of $\{1, \dots, tbruqkse\}$;
on the right, one has
\[
\binom{tbruqkse}{wirbpxsa} (qzxwvtnp\cdots mldpqrvo)^{wirbpxsa/tbruqkse} \cdots (rtyusfgh \dots iyklmwer)^{(tbruqkse-wirbpxsa)/tbruqkse},
\]
which is precisely $\binom{tbruqkse}{wirbpxsa}$ times the geometric mean of the terms
on the left.  Thus AM-GM shows that the terms under consideration on the
left exceed those on the right; adding these inequalities over all $wirbpxsa$
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Since both sides are continuous in each $xkceqbzn$, it is sufficient to
prove the claim with $qzxwvtnp, \dots, mldpqrvo$ all positive (the general case
follows by taking limits as some of the $xkceqbzn$ tend to zero).
Put $kobtuvny = szadqplm/xkceqbzn$; then the given inequality is equivalent to
\[
(1 + kobtuvny_1)^{1/tbruqkse} \cdots (1+kobtuvny_{tbruqkse})^{1/tbruqkse} \geq 1 + (kobtuvny_1\cdots kobtuvny_{tbruqkse})^{1/tbruqkse}.
\]
In terms of the function
\[
jvchiduk(qlomnves) = \log(1 + e^{qlomnves})
\]
and the quantities $plaernxz = \log kobtuvny$,
we can rewrite the desired inequality as
\[
\frac{1}{tbruqkse}(jvchiduk(plaernxz_1) + \cdots + jvchiduk(plaernxz_{tbruqkse})) \geq jvchiduk\left( \frac{plaernxz_1 + \cdots +
plaernxz_{tbruqkse}}{tbruqkse} \right).
\]
This will follow from Jensen's inequality if we can verify that $jvchiduk$
is a convex function; it is enough to check that $jvchiduk''(qlomnves) > 0$ for all $qlomnves$.
In fact,
\[
jvchiduk'(qlomnves) = \frac{e^{qlomnves}}{1+e^{qlomnves}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{qlomnves}}
\]
is an increasing function of $qlomnves$, so $jvchiduk''(qlomnves) > 0$ and Jensen's inequality
thus yields the desired result. (As long as the $qzxwvtnp$ are all positive,
equality holds when $plaernxz_1 = \cdots = plaernxz_{tbruqkse}$, i.e., when the vectors
$(qzxwvtnp, \dots, mldpqrvo)$ and $(rtyusfgh, \dots, iyklmwer)$. Of course other equality
cases crop up if some of the $qzxwvtnp$ vanish, i.e., if $qzxwvtnp=rtyusfgh=0$.)

\textbf{Fourth solution:}
We apply induction on $tbruqkse$, the case $tbruqkse=1$ being evident.
First we verify the auxiliary inequality
\[
(nfqsyhjt^{tbruqkse} + pxladrwo^{tbruqkse})(vsgkmebu^{tbruqkse} + hqntczay^{tbruqkse})^{tbruqkse-1} \geq (nfqsyhjt vsgkmebu^{tbruqkse-1} + pxladrwo hqntczay^{tbruqkse-1})^{tbruqkse}
\]
for $nfqsyhjt,pxladrwo,vsgkmebu,hqntczay \geq 0$.
The left side can be written as
\begin{align*}
nfqsyhjt^{tbruqkse} vsgkmebu^{tbruqkse(tbruqkse-1)} &+ pxladrwo^{tbruqkse} hqntczay^{tbruqkse(tbruqkse-1)} \\
&+ \sum_{ucgmzrlo=1}^{tbruqkse-1} \binom{tbruqkse-1}{ucgmzrlo} pxladrwo^{tbruqkse} vsgkmebu^{tbruqkse ucgmzrlo} hqntczay^{tbruqkse(tbruqkse-1-ucgmzrlo)} \\
&+ \sum_{ucgmzrlo=1}^{tbruqkse-1} \binom{tbruqkse-1}{ucgmzrlo-1} nfqsyhjt^{tbruqkse} vsgkmebu^{tbruqkse(ucgmzrlo-1)} hqntczay^{tbruqkse(tbruqkse-ucgmzrlo)}.
\end{align*}
Applying the weighted AM-GM inequality between matching terms in the two
sums yields
\begin{multline*}
(nfqsyhjt^{tbruqkse} + pxladrwo^{tbruqkse})(vsgkmebu^{tbruqkse} + hqntczay^{tbruqkse})^{tbruqkse-1} \geq nfqsyhjt^{tbruqkse} vsgkmebu^{tbruqkse(tbruqkse-1)} + pxladrwo^{tbruqkse} hqntczay^{tbruqkse(tbruqkse-1)} \\
+ \sum_{ucgmzrlo=1}^{tbruqkse-1} \binom{tbruqkse}{ucgmzrlo} nfqsyhjt^{ucgmzrlo} pxladrwo^{tbruqkse-ucgmzrlo} vsgkmebu^{(tbruqkse-1)ucgmzrlo} hqntczay^{(tbruqkse-1)(tbruqkse-ucgmzrlo)},
\end{multline*}
proving the auxiliary inequality.

Now given the auxiliary inequality and the $tbruqkse-1$ case of the desired
inequality, we apply the auxiliary inequality with $nfqsyhjt = qzxwvtnp^{1/tbruqkse}$,
$pxladrwo = rtyusfgh^{1/tbruqkse}$, $vsgkmebu = (hjgrksla \cdots mldpqrvo)^{1/tbruqkse(tbruqkse-1)}$,
$hqntczay = (eopncvxz \dots iyklmwer)^{1/tbruqkse(tbruqkse-1)}$. The right side will be the $tbruqkse$-th
power of the desired inequality. The left side comes out to
\[
(qzxwvtnp + rtyusfgh)((hjgrksla \cdots mldpqrvo)^{1/(tbruqkse-1)} + (eopncvxz \cdots iyklmwer)^{1/(tbruqkse-1)})^{tbruqkse-1},
\]
and by the induction hypothesis, the second factor is less than
$(hjgrksla + eopncvxz)\cdots(mldpqrvo+iyklmwer)$. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Note:}
Equality holds if and only if $xkceqbzn=szadqplm=0$ for some $ucgmzrlo$ or if the vectors
$(qzxwvtnp, \dots, mldpqrvo)$ and $(rtyusfgh, \dots, iyklmwer)$ are proportional.
As pointed out by Naoki Sato, the problem also appeared on the 1992
Irish Mathematical Olympiad.
It is also a special case of a classical inequality,
known as H"older's inequality, which generalizes the
Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (this is visible from the $tbruqkse=2$ case); the
first solution above is adapted from the standard proof of H"older's
inequality.
We don't know whether the declaration
``Apply H"older's inequality'' by itself is considered
an acceptable solution to this problem.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n,d\in\mathbb N$.  
For every $k\in\{1,\dots ,n\}$ assume  

$\bullet$ $A_k$ and $B_k$ are real $d\times d$ symmetric positive-semidefinite matrices,  

$\bullet$ the $2n$ matrices $A_1,\dots ,A_n,B_1,\dots ,B_n$ pairwise commute (so they are
simultaneously orthogonally diagonalisable).

Put  
\[
\Pi_{A}:=A_1A_2\cdots A_n,\qquad 
\Pi_{B}:=B_1B_2\cdots B_n,
\]
\[
\Delta_{A}:=\det(\Pi_{A}),\qquad 
\Delta_{B}:=\det(\Pi_{B}),\qquad 
\Delta_{A+B}:=\det\!\bigl[(A_1+B_1)(A_2+B_2)\cdots(A_n+B_n)\bigr].
\]

(a)  Prove the determinantal matrix inequality  
\[
\boxed{\;
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}\;\le\;\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}\;}
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(b)  Analyse the equality cases in the {\em scalar} situation $d=1$ and show that
\[
\text{equality in $(\star)$ for $d=1$}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\Bigl[\text{there exists }k\text{ with }A_k=B_k=0\Bigr]
\;\text{or}\;
\Bigl[\exists\lambda\ge0:\;A_k=\lambda B_k\ \text{ for every }k\Bigr].
\]

(c)  Prove that in the genuine matrix situation $d\ge 2$ the inequality in $(\star)$ is
{\em strict} whenever both determinants are strictly positive:
\[
d\ge 2,\ \Delta_{A}>0,\ \Delta_{B}>0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}<\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}.
\]

(d)  Give at least one non-trivial example with $d\ge 2$ in which equality holds
in $(\star)$ (necessarily implying $\Delta_{A}\,\Delta_{B}=0$).

(The complete description of all equality cases for $d\ge 2$ is surprisingly
intricate and is {\em not} required.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout ``$\operatorname{diag}$'' denotes a diagonal matrix with the listed
entries in the displayed order.

\textbf{Step 1 - Simultaneous diagonalisation.}  
Because the $2n$ matrices are real symmetric and pairwise commuting, there
exists an orthogonal matrix $U$ such that for each $k$
\[
U^{\mathrm T}A_kU=\Lambda_k=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_{k,1},\dots ,\lambda_{k,d}),
\qquad
U^{\mathrm T}B_kU=M_k=\operatorname{diag}(\mu_{k,1},\dots ,\mu_{k,d}),
\]
with $\lambda_{k,i},\mu_{k,i}\ge 0$.  Define
\[
a_i:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}\lambda_{k,i}\Bigr)^{1/n},\qquad
b_i:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}\mu_{k,i}\Bigr)^{1/n},\qquad
r_i:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}(\lambda_{k,i}+\mu_{k,i})\Bigr)^{1/n}.
\tag{1}
\]
Because determinants of diagonal matrices are products of their diagonal
entries,
\[
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}=\prod_{i=1}^{d}a_i,\qquad
\Delta_{B}^{1/n}=\prod_{i=1}^{d}b_i,\qquad
\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}=\prod_{i=1}^{d}r_i.
\tag{2}
\]

\textbf{Step 2 - A rowwise scalar inequality.}  
Fix an index $i\in\{1,\dots ,d\}$.  For this $i$ we have $2n$ non-negative
numbers
$\lambda_{1,i},\dots ,\lambda_{n,i},\mu_{1,i},\dots ,\mu_{n,i}$,
and the original Olympiad inequality gives  
\[
a_i+b_i\;\le\;r_i\qquad(i=1,\dots ,d).
\tag{3}
\]

\textbf{Step 3 - From the rows to the determinant.}  
Multiplying (3) over $i$ and using (2) yields  
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(a_i+b_i)\;\le\;\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}.
\tag{4}
\]
For arbitrary non-negative pairs $(x_i,y_i)$ the elementary inequality  
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(x_i+y_i)\;\ge\;\prod_{i=1}^{d}x_i+\prod_{i=1}^{d}y_i
\tag{5}
\]
holds, because the left-hand side expands to those two ``pure'' monomials
plus further non-negative mixed terms.  Taking $(x_i,y_i)=(a_i,b_i)$ and
combining (5) with (4) gives $(\star)$, completing part (a).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Step 4 - Scalar equality analysis ($d=1$).}  

Now $d=1$, so there is only one index and (5) is an equality.  Equality
in $(\star)$ is therefore equivalent to equality in the {\em scalar}
inequality (3):
\[
a_1+b_1=r_1.
\tag{6}
\]
Write $a_k:=\lambda_{k,1}$ and $b_k:=\mu_{k,1}$ $(k=1,\dots ,n)$.
Relation (6) is exactly the classical inequality
\[
A+B\le C,\qquad
A:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}a_k\Bigr)^{1/n},\;
B:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}b_k\Bigr)^{1/n},\;
C:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}(a_k+b_k)\Bigr)^{1/n},
\]
so Holder's equality criterion applies:
\[
A+B=C
\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl[\text{there exists }k\text{ with }a_k=b_k=0\Bigr]\;
\text{or}\;
\Bigl[\exists\lambda\ge 0:\,a_k=\lambda b_k\ \forall k\Bigr].
\tag{7}
\]
Translated back to the present matrix language (remember $d=1$ so
$A_k=a_k$, $B_k=b_k$) this is exactly the statement required in part (b).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Step 5 - Strictness when $d\ge 2$ and both determinants are positive.}  

Assume $d\ge 2$ and $\Delta_{A},\Delta_{B}>0$.  
Then every $a_i$ and $b_i$ defined in (1) is strictly positive.  
Consequently each factor $(a_i+b_i)$ in (5) satisfies  
$(a_i+b_i)>\max\{a_i,b_i\}$, so the inequality (5) is \emph{strict}:
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(a_i+b_i)\;>\;\prod_{i=1}^{d}a_i+\prod_{i=1}^{d}b_i
=\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}.
\tag{8}
\]
Combining (8) with the (possibly non-strict) estimate (4) we obtain
\[
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}\;<\;\prod_{i=1}^{d}(a_i+b_i)\;\le\;\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n},
\]
which is exactly the desired strict inequality in part (c).

(The crucial observation is that strictness of (5) already supplies the
necessary ``gap''; no strictness of (4) is required.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Step 6 - A sample equality example for $d\ge 2$.}  

Let $n=2$, $d=2$.  After a common diagonalisation choose  
\[
\Lambda_1=\operatorname{diag}(2,0),\quad
\Lambda_2=\operatorname{diag}(1,0),\quad
M_1=\operatorname{diag}(1,0),\quad
M_2=\operatorname{diag}(2,0).
\]
Then
\[
a_1=b_1=\sqrt 2,\qquad a_2=b_2=r_2=0,\qquad r_1=3,
\]
hence
\[
\Delta_{A}^{1/2}=0,\quad
\Delta_{B}^{1/2}=0,\quad
\Delta_{A+B}^{1/2}=0,
\]
and equality holds in $(\star)$ while neither family $\{A_k\}$ nor
$\{B_k\}$ is identically zero.  This confirms that for $d\ge 2$ equality
can occur only in degenerate situations with $\Delta_{A}\,\Delta_{B}=0$,
as asserted in part (d).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
The inequality $(\star)$ together with parts (b), (c) and (d) is now
completely proved.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimensional objects: The problem moves from scalar variables to d×d matrices; determinants replace simple products, introducing multi-linear algebra.

2. Additional structural hypotheses: Mutual commutativity and positive-semidefiniteness are required so that simultaneous diagonalisation (an advanced linear-algebraic technique) can be invoked.

3. Deeper theoretical tools:  
   • Spectral theory of commuting self–adjoint matrices.  
   • Properties of determinants under orthogonal conjugation.  
   • Lifting a scalar inequality to matrix level via eigenvalue decomposition.

4. Interacting concepts: One must blend the original AM–GM (or Hölder) idea with linear-algebraic diagonalisation and with determinant multiplicativity.

5. Richer equality analysis: Determining equality now involves both eigenvalue relations and matrix–scalar proportionality, far subtler than in the original scalar setting.

Hence the enhanced variant demands considerably more sophisticated knowledge and a multi-step argument, making it significantly harder than both the original Olympiad problem and its existing kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $n,d\in\mathbb N$.  
For every $k\in\{1,\dots ,n\}$ assume  

$\bullet$ $A_k$ and $B_k$ are real $d\times d$ symmetric positive-semidefinite matrices,  

$\bullet$ the $2n$ matrices $A_1,\dots ,A_n,B_1,\dots ,B_n$ pairwise commute (so they are
simultaneously orthogonally diagonalisable).

Put  
\[
\Pi_{A}:=A_1A_2\cdots A_n,\qquad 
\Pi_{B}:=B_1B_2\cdots B_n,
\]
\[
\Delta_{A}:=\det(\Pi_{A}),\qquad 
\Delta_{B}:=\det(\Pi_{B}),\qquad 
\Delta_{A+B}:=\det\!\bigl[(A_1+B_1)(A_2+B_2)\cdots(A_n+B_n)\bigr].
\]

(a)  Prove the determinantal matrix inequality  
\[
\boxed{\;
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}\;\le\;\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}\;}
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(b)  Analyse the equality cases in the {\em scalar} situation $d=1$ and show that
\[
\text{equality in $(\star)$ for $d=1$}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\Bigl[\text{there exists }k\text{ with }A_k=B_k=0\Bigr]
\;\text{or}\;
\Bigl[\exists\lambda\ge0:\;A_k=\lambda B_k\ \text{ for every }k\Bigr].
\]

(c)  Prove that in the genuine matrix situation $d\ge 2$ the inequality in $(\star)$ is
{\em strict} whenever both determinants are strictly positive:
\[
d\ge 2,\ \Delta_{A}>0,\ \Delta_{B}>0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}<\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}.
\]

(d)  Give at least one non-trivial example with $d\ge 2$ in which equality holds
in $(\star)$ (necessarily implying $\Delta_{A}\,\Delta_{B}=0$).

(The complete description of all equality cases for $d\ge 2$ is surprisingly
intricate and is {\em not} required.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout ``$\operatorname{diag}$'' denotes a diagonal matrix with the listed
entries in the displayed order.

\textbf{Step 1 - Simultaneous diagonalisation.}  
Because the $2n$ matrices are real symmetric and pairwise commuting, there
exists an orthogonal matrix $U$ such that for each $k$
\[
U^{\mathrm T}A_kU=\Lambda_k=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_{k,1},\dots ,\lambda_{k,d}),
\qquad
U^{\mathrm T}B_kU=M_k=\operatorname{diag}(\mu_{k,1},\dots ,\mu_{k,d}),
\]
with $\lambda_{k,i},\mu_{k,i}\ge 0$.  Define
\[
a_i:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}\lambda_{k,i}\Bigr)^{1/n},\qquad
b_i:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}\mu_{k,i}\Bigr)^{1/n},\qquad
r_i:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}(\lambda_{k,i}+\mu_{k,i})\Bigr)^{1/n}.
\tag{1}
\]
Because determinants of diagonal matrices are products of their diagonal
entries,
\[
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}=\prod_{i=1}^{d}a_i,\qquad
\Delta_{B}^{1/n}=\prod_{i=1}^{d}b_i,\qquad
\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}=\prod_{i=1}^{d}r_i.
\tag{2}
\]

\textbf{Step 2 - A rowwise scalar inequality.}  
Fix an index $i\in\{1,\dots ,d\}$.  For this $i$ we have $2n$ non-negative
numbers
$\lambda_{1,i},\dots ,\lambda_{n,i},\mu_{1,i},\dots ,\mu_{n,i}$,
and the original Olympiad inequality gives  
\[
a_i+b_i\;\le\;r_i\qquad(i=1,\dots ,d).
\tag{3}
\]

\textbf{Step 3 - From the rows to the determinant.}  
Multiplying (3) over $i$ and using (2) yields  
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(a_i+b_i)\;\le\;\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n}.
\tag{4}
\]
For arbitrary non-negative pairs $(x_i,y_i)$ the elementary inequality  
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(x_i+y_i)\;\ge\;\prod_{i=1}^{d}x_i+\prod_{i=1}^{d}y_i
\tag{5}
\]
holds, because the left-hand side expands to those two ``pure'' monomials
plus further non-negative mixed terms.  Taking $(x_i,y_i)=(a_i,b_i)$ and
combining (5) with (4) gives $(\star)$, completing part (a).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Step 4 - Scalar equality analysis ($d=1$).}  

Now $d=1$, so there is only one index and (5) is an equality.  Equality
in $(\star)$ is therefore equivalent to equality in the {\em scalar}
inequality (3):
\[
a_1+b_1=r_1.
\tag{6}
\]
Write $a_k:=\lambda_{k,1}$ and $b_k:=\mu_{k,1}$ $(k=1,\dots ,n)$.
Relation (6) is exactly the classical inequality
\[
A+B\le C,\qquad
A:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}a_k\Bigr)^{1/n},\;
B:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}b_k\Bigr)^{1/n},\;
C:=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}(a_k+b_k)\Bigr)^{1/n},
\]
so Holder's equality criterion applies:
\[
A+B=C
\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl[\text{there exists }k\text{ with }a_k=b_k=0\Bigr]\;
\text{or}\;
\Bigl[\exists\lambda\ge 0:\,a_k=\lambda b_k\ \forall k\Bigr].
\tag{7}
\]
Translated back to the present matrix language (remember $d=1$ so
$A_k=a_k$, $B_k=b_k$) this is exactly the statement required in part (b).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Step 5 - Strictness when $d\ge 2$ and both determinants are positive.}  

Assume $d\ge 2$ and $\Delta_{A},\Delta_{B}>0$.  
Then every $a_i$ and $b_i$ defined in (1) is strictly positive.  
Consequently each factor $(a_i+b_i)$ in (5) satisfies  
$(a_i+b_i)>\max\{a_i,b_i\}$, so the inequality (5) is \emph{strict}:
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(a_i+b_i)\;>\;\prod_{i=1}^{d}a_i+\prod_{i=1}^{d}b_i
=\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}.
\tag{8}
\]
Combining (8) with the (possibly non-strict) estimate (4) we obtain
\[
\Delta_{A}^{1/n}+\Delta_{B}^{1/n}\;<\;\prod_{i=1}^{d}(a_i+b_i)\;\le\;\Delta_{A+B}^{1/n},
\]
which is exactly the desired strict inequality in part (c).

(The crucial observation is that strictness of (5) already supplies the
necessary ``gap''; no strictness of (4) is required.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Step 6 - A sample equality example for $d\ge 2$.}  

Let $n=2$, $d=2$.  After a common diagonalisation choose  
\[
\Lambda_1=\operatorname{diag}(2,0),\quad
\Lambda_2=\operatorname{diag}(1,0),\quad
M_1=\operatorname{diag}(1,0),\quad
M_2=\operatorname{diag}(2,0).
\]
Then
\[
a_1=b_1=\sqrt 2,\qquad a_2=b_2=r_2=0,\qquad r_1=3,
\]
hence
\[
\Delta_{A}^{1/2}=0,\quad
\Delta_{B}^{1/2}=0,\quad
\Delta_{A+B}^{1/2}=0,
\]
and equality holds in $(\star)$ while neither family $\{A_k\}$ nor
$\{B_k\}$ is identically zero.  This confirms that for $d\ge 2$ equality
can occur only in degenerate situations with $\Delta_{A}\,\Delta_{B}=0$,
as asserted in part (d).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
The inequality $(\star)$ together with parts (b), (c) and (d) is now
completely proved.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

2. Additional structural hypotheses: Mutual commutativity and positive-semidefiniteness are required so that simultaneous diagonalisation (an advanced linear-algebraic technique) can be invoked.
